content.text: Obtaining a certification is the beginning, not the end, of a compliance investment. SOC 2 Type II audits repeat annually. ISO 42001 and ISO 27001 require annual surveillance audits in years two and three and a full recertification audit in year four. Without a deliberate maintenance cadence, organizations find themselves scrambling each year to assemble evidence that should have been collected continuously, revisiting control gaps that were never fully closed, and managing audit scope creep as AI systems evolve faster than the governance program that covers them.

A maintenance cadence is a calendar of recurring activities — monthly, quarterly, annual, and event-triggered — that keeps the compliance program current without requiring heroic effort each audit cycle. It ensures evidence is collected in the right format as it is generated, control owners remain engaged between audits, and significant changes to the AI program (new models, new use cases, regulatory updates) are reflected in the control environment before an auditor asks about them.

attrs.alt: AI certification maintenance cadence showing SOC 2 annual audit cycle and ISO 42001 3-year cycle with monthly, quarterly, annual, and on-change maintenance activities

content.text: Why Maintenance Cadence Matters

The most common reason organizations receive audit findings in year two of a SOC 2 engagement is not that controls deteriorated — it is that evidence collection was not maintained. Logs were rotated before the audit period ended. Access reviews were not documented. Model cards were not updated after retraining. These are process failures, not control failures, and they are entirely preventable with a structured cadence.

For AI-specific certifications, the maintenance challenge is compounded by the pace of change. AI systems are retrained more frequently than traditional software is updated. New AI capabilities are deployed without formal change management. The population of affected individuals changes as deployment scales. Each of these changes is a potential scope expansion or control gap that the maintenance cadence must capture.
Benefits
Reduces audit preparation time by keeping evidence current throughout the year rather than assembling it in a pre-audit sprint.
Maintains control owner engagement between audits — monthly check-ins prevent the 'we forgot we own this control' problem.
Surfaces drift between the documented control environment and actual operations while there is still time to remediate before auditors arrive.
Change-triggered reviews ensure scope changes from new AI deployments are processed through governance before they become audit findings.
Builds institutional memory — documented cadence activities create a history that survives personnel turnover.
Trade-offs
The cadence consumes ongoing time from control owners who have other responsibilities; without executive support, recurring activities get deprioritized.
Monthly activities can become perfunctory checkbox-filling rather than genuine reviews if the cadence is not actively managed.
For organizations early in their compliance journey, establishing the cadence is itself a significant project requiring dedicated program management.
Core Maintenance Activities by Frequency
Monthly Activities

Monthly activities are lightweight and focused on hygiene: verifying that evidence is being collected correctly, checking in with control owners on the status of open action items, and reviewing the model card inventory for any systems retrained or updated since the last check. The monthly review should take no more than two hours per person and should be calendar-blocked by the compliance program manager.
Evidence hygiene check: verify that log retention is operating, monitoring alerts are being reviewed within SLA, and access review schedules are being met.
Open finding review: check the status of any open internal audit findings or mock audit findings; escalate anything approaching its due date.
Model card currency check: confirm that model cards for all in-scope AI systems reflect the current production version.
New AI system intake: review the AI system intake queue for any new systems that may need to be brought into certification scope.
Quarterly Activities

Quarterly activities are more substantive and include structured reviews. The quarterly access review is particularly important for AI systems because access to model inference endpoints and training data pipelines changes frequently as engineering teams grow and reorganize.
Access review: pull the access list for all in-scope AI systems and verify that each user has a current business justification.
Risk register review: assess whether any risk ratings need adjustment based on new information — new regulatory guidance, industry incidents, internal operational data.
Vendor assessment: review the security and compliance posture of any LLM API providers or AI platform vendors in the supply chain.
Internal audit sample: test a sample of controls with the same methodology used in the annual internal audit to maintain auditor readiness.
Training completion: confirm that required AI governance training has been completed by all staff in relevant roles.
Annual Activities

Annual activities are the most intensive and are typically organized around the audit cycle. The full internal audit (conducted prior to the external audit) tests all in-scope controls and produces the findings register that drives remediation planning. The management review is a formal executive-level meeting required by ISO 42001 Clause 9 and represents the governance program's annual accountability event.
Full internal audit: test all in-scope controls, document findings, and issue the internal audit report.
Management review: formal meeting with executive sponsors reviewing AIMS performance, risk profile, objective achievement, and improvement priorities.
AI policy and procedure refresh: review all governance policies for accuracy and relevance; update to reflect regulatory changes and organizational learning.
Annual training renewal: refresh AI governance training for all staff, incorporating lessons from the past year's incidents and audit findings.
Scope review: evaluate whether the certification scope is still appropriate — new AI systems may need to be added; deprecated systems removed.
Change-Triggered Activities

Change-triggered activities are initiated by events rather than the calendar. They are the hardest to maintain consistently because they require the AI program to have a change notification mechanism — engineering and product teams must notify the compliance function when they make changes that affect the audit scope.
New AI system deployment: trigger a risk assessment and impact assessment; determine whether the new system should be added to certification scope; update model cards and system cards before go-live.
Model retraining: log the retraining as a change management event; capture the change ticket, test results, and approval sign-off as audit evidence.
Significant model behavior change: if a model update materially changes accuracy, safety properties, or capabilities, trigger a re-evaluation of the risk register and any relevant impact assessments.
Regulatory update: when a new regulation or regulatory guidance is issued that affects in-scope AI systems, assess the impact on controls and update the risk register accordingly.
Audit finding remediation: when a control owner reports a finding as remediated, trigger a re-test and update the finding status in the register.
Mental Model: The Maintenance Cadence as a Heartbeat

A maintenance cadence is the heartbeat of the compliance program. Monthly activities are the rapid heartbeat — frequent, light, keeping the program alive. Quarterly activities are the breathing — slower but more substantive, renewing oxygen across the control environment. Annual activities are the health checkup — comprehensive, evaluative, identifying anything that has gone wrong over the year. Change-triggered activities are the immune response — activated when the body encounters something new or threatening. A healthy compliance program has all four rhythms operating in sync.
Implementation: Maintenance Calendar Template

# ai-certification-maintenance-calendar.yaml
# Annual maintenance schedule for SOC 2 + ISO 42001 AI governance certifications.
# Adapt months to your organization's audit cycle; this example assumes Q4 external audit.

annual_cycle:
  audit_target: Q4 (October external audit)
  certification_in_scope: [SOC2_TypeII, ISO42001]

monthly_recurring:
  timing: "First Monday of each month"
  owner: Compliance Program Manager
  activities:
    - evidence_hygiene_check
    - open_finding_review
    - model_card_currency_check
    - new_system_intake_review
  output: Monthly compliance status report (1 page)

quarterly_recurring:
  Q1:  # January - March
    activities:
      - access_review_all_ai_systems
      - risk_register_review
      - vendor_assessment_update
      - internal_control_sample_Q1
    output: Q1 Compliance Health Report
  Q2:  # April - June
    activities:
      - access_review_all_ai_systems
      - training_completion_check
      - risk_register_review
      - mock_audit_execution          # 6 months before Oct audit
      - internal_control_sample_Q2
    output: Mock Audit Findings Report, Q2 Health Report
  Q3:  # July - September
    activities:
      - access_review_all_ai_systems
      - risk_register_review
      - pre_audit_evidence_assembly   # prepare evidence package
      - internal_audit_full           # 8 weeks before external audit
      - management_review_pre_audit
      - internal_control_sample_Q3
    output: Internal Audit Report, Pre-Audit Evidence Package
  Q4:  # October - December
    activities:
      - external_audit_support        # fieldwork support
      - finding_remediation_tracking
      - access_review_all_ai_systems
      - annual_policy_refresh
      - annual_training_renewal
      - scope_review_for_next_year
      - management_review_annual
    output: Certification Letter, Annual Compliance Report

change_triggered:
  new_ai_system:
    trigger: "AI system deployed to production"
    sla_hours: 72
    activities: [risk_assessment, impact_assessment, model_card_creation, scope_assessment]
  model_retraining:
    trigger: "Model retrained and promoted to production"
    sla_hours: 24
    activities: [change_ticket_creation, test_result_logging, approval_sign_off_capture]
  regulatory_update:
    trigger: "New regulation or guidance affecting AI systems"
    sla_days: 30
    activities: [impact_assessment, control_gap_analysis, risk_register_update]
  audit_finding_closed:
    trigger: "Control owner marks finding as remediated"
    sla_days: 14
    activities: [retest_execution, retest_evidence_collection, finding_status_update]
Code Example: Change-Triggered Notification System

"""
Lightweight change notification system that alerts the compliance team
when AI system changes occur that may affect certification scope.
Integrates with a deployment pipeline or CI/CD system.
"""

import json, datetime, smtplib
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Literal

ChangeType = Literal["new_system", "model_retraining", "scope_change", "regulatory_update"]

@dataclass
class ComplianceChangeEvent:
    event_id: str
    change_type: ChangeType
    ai_system: str
    description: str
    changed_by: str
    change_date: datetime.date
    sla_days: int
    required_actions: list[str]
    notified_at: datetime.datetime = None

    def __post_init__(self):
        self.notified_at = datetime.datetime.utcnow()

    def due_date(self) -> datetime.date:
        return self.change_date + datetime.timedelta(days=self.sla_days)

SLA_BY_CHANGE_TYPE = {
    "new_system": 3,
    "model_retraining": 1,
    "scope_change": 7,
    "regulatory_update": 30,
}

REQUIRED_ACTIONS_BY_CHANGE_TYPE = {
    "new_system": [
        "Complete AI system risk assessment",
        "Complete algorithmic impact assessment",
        "Create model card",
        "Determine certification scope inclusion",
    ],
    "model_retraining": [
        "Capture change ticket reference",
        "Log test results for retrained model",
        "Capture approval sign-off",
        "Update model card version",
    ],
    "scope_change": [
        "Update Statement of Applicability (ISO 42001)",
        "Notify external auditor if in active audit window",
        "Reassess risk register",
    ],
    "regulatory_update": [
        "Assess impact on in-scope AI systems",
        "Update risk register with new regulatory risk",
        "Identify control gaps against new requirement",
        "Update AI governance policy if needed",
    ],
}

def create_change_event(
    change_type: ChangeType,
    ai_system: str,
    description: str,
    changed_by: str,
    event_id: str = None,
) -> ComplianceChangeEvent:
    return ComplianceChangeEvent(
        event_id=event_id or f"CHG-{datetime.date.today().strftime('%Y%m%d')}-001",
        change_type=change_type,
        ai_system=ai_system,
        description=description,
        changed_by=changed_by,
        change_date=datetime.date.today(),
        sla_days=SLA_BY_CHANGE_TYPE[change_type],
        required_actions=REQUIRED_ACTIONS_BY_CHANGE_TYPE[change_type],
    )

def notify_compliance_team(event: ComplianceChangeEvent, compliance_email: str):
    body = f"""AI Compliance Change Notification

Change Type:   {event.change_type}
AI System:     {event.ai_system}
Description:   {event.description}
Changed By:    {event.changed_by}
Change Date:   {event.change_date}
SLA Due Date:  {event.due_date()}

Required Actions:
{chr(10).join(f"  - {a}" for a in event.required_actions)}

Please complete all required actions within the SLA window.
Reference: {event.event_id}
"""
    msg = MIMEText(body)
    msg["Subject"] = f"[AI Compliance] {event.change_type.upper()} — {event.ai_system}"
    msg["From"] = "ai-compliance-system@company.com"
    msg["To"] = compliance_email

    # In production: configure SMTP server
    # with smtplib.SMTP("smtp.company.com") as smtp:
    #     smtp.send_message(msg)
    print(f"Notification prepared for {compliance_email}:
{body}")

# Example usage (called from CI/CD pipeline on model promotion)
event = create_change_event(
    change_type="model_retraining",
    ai_system="Credit Underwriting Agent v2.4",
    description="Model retrained with Q2 2026 data; accuracy improved from 91.2% to 93.1%",
    changed_by="ml-pipeline-prod",
    event_id="CHG-20260719-001",
)
notify_compliance_team(event, "ai-compliance@company.com")
print(json.dumps(asdict(event), default=str, indent=2))
Real-World Example: FinTech Compliance Team

A fintech company with a SOC 2 Type II certification for their AI loan origination platform developed a compliance maintenance calendar after their Year 2 audit returned three findings related to evidence gaps. In Year 1, evidence collection had been a manual scramble in the weeks before the audit. For Year 2 they implemented: automated monthly evidence exports from their monitoring platform to an evidence repository; a shared calendar with quarterly access reviews blocked for all control owners; and a Slack integration that posted to the #ai-compliance channel whenever a model was promoted to production, triggering the change-triggered review workflow. The Year 3 audit passed with zero findings, and preparation time dropped from six weeks to two weeks because evidence was current and organized throughout the year.
Common Mistakes
Letting the annual audit cycle be the only compliance activity — evidence gaps accumulate silently throughout the year.
Failing to notify the compliance team when models are retrained — this is the most common change-management gap in AI programs.
Treating the management review as an informal meeting rather than a formal documented session — auditors will request meeting minutes and attendance records.
Expanding AI system scope mid-audit-period without notifying the auditor — this creates scope confusion and may invalidate evidence collected before the change.
Allowing model cards to drift out of date because there is no change-triggered update process — outdated model cards are a frequent P2 or P3 finding.
Best Practices
Build the maintenance calendar into your project management or GRC tool so activities appear as recurring tasks, not as items that need to be remembered.
Assign each recurring activity a named owner and a backup owner — activities with single-point-of-ownership dependency fail when the owner changes roles.
Automate evidence collection wherever possible — log exports, configuration snapshots, and access lists should populate the evidence repository on a schedule, not on-demand during audit preparation.
Brief new AI engineers on change notification requirements during onboarding — compliance obligations must be part of engineering culture, not discovered retroactively.
Negotiate with your auditor on the evidence format and export schedule at the start of each audit cycle — knowing what they will accept prevents re-collection in a different format.
Use the surveillance audit as a calibration point: treat surveillance finding themes as early warning signals for the recertification audit.
Performance and Scalability

A maintenance cadence for a single AI system with 30 controls can be managed with a few hours of effort per month. As the AI portfolio grows to 10+ systems across multiple regulatory regimes, a dedicated compliance program manager and supporting GRC tooling become necessary. At scale, automated evidence collection is not optional — it is the difference between a sustainable program and a team that is perpetually in audit preparation mode.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Effort

Structured maintenance cadence (this pattern)

High — continuous

Security and Reliability

The evidence repository itself is a compliance-critical asset. Evidence artifacts must be stored in a write-once or immutable format where the collection timestamp is verifiable. If evidence can be retroactively modified, auditors cannot rely on it. Use systems with cryptographic hashing of artifacts at collection time and audit trails showing who accessed or attempted to modify evidence files.
Key Takeaways
A maintenance cadence is a calendar of recurring compliance activities (monthly, quarterly, annual, change-triggered) that keeps certification current without pre-audit sprints.
Monthly activities focus on hygiene: evidence currency, open finding status, model card accuracy, and new system intake.
Quarterly activities include structured reviews: access review, risk register review, vendor assessment, and internal control sampling.
Annual activities include the full internal audit, formal management review, policy refresh, and scope reassessment.
Change-triggered activities are the hardest to maintain: new AI deployments and model retrainings must notify the compliance team automatically, ideally through CI/CD pipeline integration.
Automated evidence collection from monitoring platforms reduces audit preparation time and improves evidence quality.
Maintenance cadence scales through GRC tooling and automation — manual processes cannot sustain compliance across a large AI portfolio.
